domain: Busy Beaver Challenge deciders: Finite Automata Reduction (FAR) and Weighted FAR (WFAR) — regular and one-counter-weighted inductive invariants for Turing machine non-halting, their Coq verifiers (23 FAR + 17 WFAR = 40 hardcoded certificates in Coq-BB5), and their SAT/enumeration search procedures

translations.technique: FAR (Finite Automata Reduction): Theorem 4.9's eight Boolean-matrix conditions + DFA-seed certificates recompleted by saturation (direct.rs / Verifier_FAR.v)
translations.what_it_actually_does: The verified kernel (decider-finite-automata-reduction/src/core/proof.rs; BB5 paper Thm 4.9) checks a certificate consisting of: a scan direction, a DFA (reads the half-tape behind the head, must satisfy delta(q0,0)=q0), an NFA over Boolean transition matrices T_0, T_1 whose state space embeds DFAstate x TMstate via nfa_start(q,f)=5q+f, an accept column vector a, and a 'steady state' row vector s. Exactly eight conditions are verified as Boolean matrix (in)equalities: (4.6) q0*T0=q0 (leading zeros ignored); (4.7) T0*a=a (trailing zeros ignored); (4.8) s*a=1; (4.9) s*T0>=s and s*T1>=s (s is absorbing); (4.10) for every halting pair (f,r) and every reachable row q0*T_u: q0*T_u*T_f*T_r >= s (finitely checkable because {q0*T_u} is a finite set); (4.11) for each left rule delta(f,r)=(w,L,t), forall b: T_b*T_f*T_r >= T_t*T_b*T_w entrywise; (4.12) for each right rule delta(f,r)=(w,R,t): T_f*T_r >= T_w*T_t; (4.13) q0*T_A*a=0 (initial config rejected). In code, closed() implements 4.10-4.12 as nfa.step comparisons of bit-vector rows. Crucially the shipped certificate (DVF file, and the 23 hardcoded Coq certificates in Verifier_FAR_Hardcoded_Certificates.v) contains ONLY the precursor DFA (a nested list like DNV 12 (DFA_from_list ...)); the NFA half is recomputed deterministically by a saturation algorithm (direct.rs: close an initially empty transition relation under rules 'halt pair maps to HALT sink', 'right rule appends', 'left rule composes pre-computed transitions') and then the completed proof is checked against the 8 conditions.
translations.math_translation: Theorem (FAR, restated): Let M be a TM with partial transition function delta: S x {0,1} -> {0,1} x {L,R} x S and start state A; encode each finite-support configuration as a word u f r v over {0,1} u S. Suppose there exist n in N, Boolean matrices T_g in B^{n x n} for each letter g, vectors q0 = e_1, a in B^n, s in B^{1 x n} satisfying the eight conditions above (with the two universally-quantified families: forall u in {0,1}* for halting closure, forall b in {0,1} for left rules). Then M does not halt from the all-zero tape. Structural reading: u |-> q0*T_u is a monoid morphism from tape words into the ordered monoid (B^{n x n}, *, <=); conditions (4.11)-(4.12) state the morphism is weakly monotone with respect to the one-step rewriting preorder (the matrix image of a configuration dominates that of its successor: 'co-CTL' = the recognized language is closed under rewriting PREimages); (4.8)-(4.10) send halting words above an absorbing accept threshold; (4.13) keeps the initial word below it. So FAR is precisely a weakly-monotone interpretation of the TM's string rewriting system into the semiring of Boolean matrices, certifying non-reachability (safety) — the order-dual of Hofbauer-Waldmann matrix interpretations, which certify termination via strictly-decreasing interpretations. Equivalently: a regular inductive invariant (the complement language) for the TM step relation, with inductiveness strengthened from language level to transition-monoid level so it becomes 8 finite matrix checks. The saturation completion in direct.rs is the Bouajjani-Esparza-Maler pre*-saturation for the pushdown system obtained by quotienting the left half-tape by the DFA's right-congruence (the README derives exactly this, citing BEM97). The companion paper [arXiv 2504.20563, 'Turing machines deciders part I'] additionally proves a universality/normal-form lemma: every regular co-CTL proof can be re-expressed in the Theorem 4.9 format.

nearest_known_math.theory: Regular model checking / regular inductive invariants for infinite-state systems (Bouajjani-Jonsson-Nilsson-Touili 2000; Abdulla et al.)
nearest_known_math.relationship: subsumed-by: a FAR certificate is a regular inductive invariant for a string rewriting relation, presented in complemented time-reversed form; the novelty is only the strengthened finite kernel (monoid-level inequalities) and the TM specialization
nearest_known_math.ref: Bouajjani, Jonsson, Nilsson, Touili, 'Regular Model Checking', CAV 2000

nearest_known_math.relationship: overlaps (knowingly - the decider README cites and re-derives it): direct.rs is pre*-saturation for the DFA-quotiented two-stack machine; FAR turns the (uncomputable for TMs) exact pre* into a certified guess-and-check

nearest_known_math.theory: Matrix interpretations and weighted-automata methods for termination of (string) rewriting; match-bounds
nearest_known_math.relationship: overlaps: identical algebra (letters -> matrices over a semiring, compatibility inequalities per rewrite rule), dual property (FAR: safety/non-reachability with >=; termination: strict decrease). FAR is the Boolean-semiring safety corner of this ladder; nobody on either side cites the other

nearest_known_math.theory: Simulation relations / Boolean matrix precongruences on NFAs
nearest_known_math.relationship: subsumed-by: the elementwise matrix order and product inequalities are standard simulation-style sufficient conditions for language inclusion
nearest_known_math.ref: PlanetMath matrix characterization of automata; standard simulation preorder literature (Milner; Lynch-Vaandrager)

translations.fusion_opportunity: The generalization axis FAR exposes is the SEMIRING. FAR = Boolean semiring; the termination community has already built the rest of the ladder for the dual (termination) problem: N-matrix interpretations, arctic (max-plus) interpretations, all SAT/SMT-searchable and certified in Isabelle/CeTA. Fusion: import that machinery with the inequality direction flipped to get 'counting FAR' (weights in N count occurrences; certificates prove quantitative invariants like 'pattern count never decreases') and 'arctic FAR' (max-plus matrices; certificates track maxima/records — natural for machines whose halting is gated by a record value). Search reuses the existing SAT encoding for the Boolean skeleton plus SMT(LIA) for weights. Conversely, exporting the bbchallenge trick to termination: the DFA-seed + verified-recompletion architecture (certificate = generator of a fixpoint, not the fixpoint) would shrink CeTA-style matrix-interpretation certificates.
translations.collatz_application: Yolcu-Aaronson-Heule (CADE 2021, arXiv 2105.14697) already do the exact termination-dual for Collatz: they encode Collatz variants as string rewriting and SAT-search matrix/arctic interpretations to prove termination. FAR supplies the missing safety half for the same encodings: a FAR certificate over the Collatz rewriting system (binary LSB-first, or their ternary encoding) is a regular set closed under inverse Collatz steps containing all configurations that reach 1 and excluding a start set S — proving 'no element of S ever reaches 1'. For Collatz itself such a certificate cannot exist if the conjecture is true, but for Collatz-like maps (5n+1, residue-restricted variants, and the certificate lemmas our program needs: 'regular family R never reaches configuration set H') it is directly searchable with the mitm_dfa SAT encoding, and the Coq verifier architecture (seed DFA + saturation + 8 checks) ports verbatim since it never uses anything TM-specific beyond the rewrite rules being 2-local.
sources_read: web: arXiv 2504.20563 abstract/structure; Hofbauer-Waldmann and match-bounds search results

translations.technique: WFAR (Weighted FAR / MITM-WDFA): Theorem 4.10 — two-sided deterministic Z-weighted automata with per-tuple weight intervals, sign-definite state sets, and widening-based closure (Iijil1/mitmwfar verifier.go; Coq Verifier_WFAR.v)
translations.what_it_actually_does: Certificate (Go full form): (1) left and right deterministic weighted automata (DWFA) over {0,1} with integer edge weights, satisfying delta(start,0)=start with weight 0; (2) 'special sets' per automaton: NonNegative and NonPositive state subsets verified backward-closed (transitionRetainsSpecialSets: an edge into a NonPositive state must come from a NonPositive state with weight <= 0, dually for NonNegative) — implying every word reaching such a state has accumulated weight of that sign; (3) an accept set mapping each control tuple (TM state, head symbol, left DWFA state, right DWFA state) to ONE integer interval [lower, upper] (either bound optional). Verification (verifier.go): start tuple accepted with 0 in its interval; no tuple with undefined TM transition is in the accept set; forward closure: for each accepted tuple and TM rule, enumerate all one-symbol pre-images on the receding side (nextConfigsWithWeightChange — for a left move, all left-DWFA edges into the current left state, paired with pushing the written symbol into the right DWFA; weight change Delta = weight(pushed edge) - weight(popped edge)), shift the interval by Delta, tighten it against the sign-set-derived feasible interval ([0,inf) if both end states NonNegative, (-inf,0] if both NonPositive), and require the result to be empty (infeasible) or included in the successor tuple's accept interval. The Coq verifier (Verifier_WFAR.v) is the same theorem with a different constraint representation: abstract states (l,r,m,s,d,g) with g in {None, Some Dpos, Some Dneg} denote exact weight d or half-lines {d, d+-1, d+-2,...}; MITM_WDFA_ES_simplify is a widening operator (when |d| >= max_d, replace the point by a half-line; certificate = just the two WDFAs from WDFA_from_list plus threshold max_d, e.g. 'WA 2 3 (WDFA_from_list ((0,0;2,0)::(1,0;1,0)::(3,1;2,0)::(1,0;2,0)::nil)) ...'); the accept set is recomputed as a terminating closure (T_decider) and MITM_WDFA_ES_good prunes sign-infeasible states via wdfa_sgn_closed. Footnote 49 of the BB5 paper is accurate: NO implementation runs Bellman-Ford; both use only the sign-definite fragment of per-state feasible weight ranges. All 17 hardcoded Coq certificates use edge weights in {-1,0,1} only; the search (decider.go) enumerates base DFA pairs closed under TM transitions in TNF-like dead-state order and perturbs one left/right transition pair by (+1,-1).
translations.math_translation: Theorem (WFAR, restated with quantifiers): Let M be a TM. Suppose there exist complete DWFAs (Q_L, delta_L, w_L: Q_L x Sigma -> Z, p0) and (Q_R, delta_R, w_R, q0) with delta_L(p0,0)=p0, w_L(p0,0)=0 (resp. right), a map C from control tuples x = (p,f,r,q) in Q_L x S x Sigma x Q_R to subsets of Z drawn from {intervals} (Go) or {finite unions of points and half-lines} (Coq), and backward-closed sign labelings certifying per-state weight signs, such that: (1) 0 in C(p0, A, 0, q0); (2) for every configuration c = (u, f, r, v) with W(c) := w_L*(u) + w_R*(rev v) in C(ctrl(c)), every TM step c -> c' satisfies W(c') in C(ctrl(c')) — verified finitely by quantifying over tuples x, applicable rules, and one-symbol pre-image branches, using the shift-and-include check modulo sign-derived infeasibility; (3) C(x) = empty for every halting x. Then M never halts from blank tape. Three exact identifications: (a) LANGUAGE CLASS: the invariant {c : W(c) in C(ctrl(c))} is recognized by an unambiguous Z-weighted automaton with semilinear (interval) acceptance = a deterministic Parikh automaton of dimension 1 = a blind one-counter (Greibach) language; notably WFAR is the ONLY non-regular invariant class in the entire BB5 proof. (b) ABSTRACT INTERPRETATION: the Coq closure is a least-fixpoint computation in the abstract domain (control tuple -> weight constraint) where the weight constraints form the interval domain over Z and MITM_WDFA_ES_simplify is threshold widening at |d| >= max_d (Cousot-Cousot, verbatim). (c) PATH-COMPLETE CERTIFICATE — answering the target question precisely: YES, up to one substitution. Take nodes = control tuples x; modes/edges = (TM rule, pre-image branch) pairs, each acting on the counter by a pure translation W -> W + Delta_e; the graph is path-complete by construction (automata are complete and pops enumerate all pre-images, so every TM trajectory lifts to a path). Per-node certificate functions are affine with slope exactly +-1: B_x(W) = W - m_x >= 0 and B'_x(W) = M_x - W >= 0; the edge conditions reduce to difference constraints m_x' <= m_x + Delta_e, M_x' >= M_x + Delta_e, i.e. the bound vectors (m_x), (M_x) are sub-/super-fixed-points of a min-plus (resp. max-plus) linear operator — feasibility of such systems IS Bellman-Ford / existence of node potentials, which is where the min-plus (tropical) structure genuinely lives; the weight accumulation itself is over the group (Z,+), not min-plus, because the automata are deterministic (no aggregation over runs). So: WFAR = path-complete barrier certificate with integer affine unit-slope templates in ONE counter, translation-only mode dynamics, plus one ingredient absent from the control-theoretic formulation: the scalar 'state' W is not given with the system but is itself synthesized, as the weight function of an automaton reading the configuration.

nearest_known_math.theory: Path-complete Lyapunov functions (Ahmadi-Jungers-Parrilo-Roozbehani) and path-complete barrier functions
nearest_known_math.relationship: overlaps: WFAR is the translation-dynamics, single-counter, unit-slope-affine, safety instance of the framework (barrier, not Lyapunov: it separates reachable from halting configurations); PC theory assumes a given vector state and searches templates by SDP, WFAR synthesizes the state map itself and searches by enumeration + closure. Neither community cites the other
nearest_known_math.ref: AJPR, SIAM J. Control Optim. 2014, arXiv:1111.3427; 'On the Completeness and Ordering of Path-Complete Barrier Functions', arXiv:2503.19561

nearest_known_math.theory: Parikh automata / blind (multi)counter automata / weighted automata over (Z^k,+)
nearest_known_math.relationship: subsumed-by: the invariant language class is exactly deterministic (two-sided unambiguous) dimension-1 Parikh automata with interval constraint sets; equivalences to Greibach blind counters and reversal-bounded counters are classical
nearest_known_math.ref: Klaedtke-Rueß ICALP 2003; Greibach TCS 1978; Cadilhac-Finkel-McKenzie 'Affine Parikh automata'; survey arXiv:2307.07238

nearest_known_math.theory: Interval abstract domain with threshold widening (abstract interpretation)
nearest_known_math.relationship: subsumed-by: MITM_WDFA_ES_simplify + T_decider closure is literally lfp computation with widening at threshold max_d; the Go 'switch to unbounded intervals after length exceeds 1000' heuristic is the same widening

nearest_known_math.theory: Difference constraint systems / shortest-path node potentials / tropical sub-eigenvectors; distance automata
nearest_known_math.relationship: subsumed-by: the feasibility layer (per-state feasible weight ranges, sign-definite sets, the paper's Bellman-Ford remark) is shortest-path potential theory; the general per-state min/max path weight computation is the trivial fragment of distance-automata theory (Hashiguchi, Simon, Krob)
nearest_known_math.ref: Cormen et al. difference constraints & Bellman-Ford; Simon, 'On semigroups of matrices over the tropical semiring', 1994

nearest_known_math.theory: Arctic / matrix interpretations for rewriting termination
nearest_known_math.relationship: overlaps: same object (automaton weights compatible with rewrite rules), dual property; WFAR's additive Z-weights correspond to 1x1 integer matrix interpretations used for safety instead of reduction orders

translations.fusion_opportunity: Spelling out the generalized certificate. Step 1 (LP level, immediate): replace scalar weights by vector weights w: Q x Sigma -> Z^k (deterministic k-dimensional Parikh automaton per side); accept sets per control tuple become rational polyhedra {y in R^2k : G_x y >= h_x}; forward closure becomes polyhedral inclusion (P_x + Delta_e) ∩ Feas_x' ⊆ P_x' — checkable by LP/Farkas, with Farkas multipliers stored in the certificate so the Coq verifier only checks linear identities (Bellman-Ford/difference constraints are exactly the k=1, two-variables-per-inequality special case). Per-state feasibility Feas becomes a polyhedral overapproximation of each state's Parikh image (computed by Kleene iteration with widening, generalizing the sign sets). Step 2 (SDP level): accept sets semialgebraic {y : B_x(y) >= 0}, closure implications certified by Positivstellensatz: B_x'(y + Delta_e) - sigma_e(y) B_x(y) - sum_i lambda_i(y) g_i(y) is SOS with sigma, lambda SOS — searched by SDP over template coefficients, rationalized, then verified exactly (validsdp-style rational SOS check integrates with Coq-BB5's reflective architecture). Honest caveat from the correspondence: with translation-only counter updates, quadratic templates help only via nonconvex/multi-counter interactions (e.g. machines maintaining x_L ~ x_R^2 — parabolic space-time envelopes; the certificate 2x_L - x_R^2 + x_R in [0, c] has SOS-checkable closure under unit translations). The deeper fusion is Step 3: let weights take values in matrices (weights in N^{dxd} or arctic matrices) — then each mode acts LINEARLY on the vector carried by the automaton and the full AJPR machinery (per-node quadratics/CQLF, SOS hierarchies, De Bruijn graph expansions — which mitmwfar's -m flag already implements without knowing it) applies verbatim; FAR (Boolean matrices) and WFAR (scalars, additively) are the two degenerate corners of this one ladder, whose middle — SDP-searched per-node quadratic certificates over matrix-weighted automatic abstractions — bbchallenge has never tried and is the concrete new tool for BB(6) holdouts with polynomial envelopes.
translations.collatz_application: For Collatz proper and our certificate program: encode the accelerated map T (odd n -> (3n+1)/2, even n -> n/2) as LSB-side rewriting on binary strings. A WFAR certificate is then: DWFA pair reading the digit string on both sides of the carry position, with a shared counter, accept intervals per (automaton states, local digits) tuple, forward-closed under T — proving statements 'the T-orbit of every n in automaton-definable set S avoids automaton-definable set H' (e.g. cycle exclusion within a definable family, or lower-bound invariants 'orbit never drops below f(n)' with f automaton-linear). The right weight structure for Collatz is 2-dimensional: weights in Z^2 tracking (a,b) = (odd steps taken, halvings taken), so the multiplicative bookkeeping 3^a/2^b becomes ADDITIVE in (a,b); the transcendental comparison 3^a vs 2^b needed by accept sets enters only through rational slope bounds p/q on log2(3) (Stern-Brocot approximants), keeping every closure check in integer linear arithmetic — i.e. a dimension-2 Parikh/WFAR certificate with polyhedral accept sets is the natural 'Collatz WFAR', one LP-level step up from what bbchallenge shipped, and searchable by SAT-modulo-difference-logic (structure Booleans + weight/bound integers) rather than mitmwfar's blind +-1 enumeration. Michel (1993, 2015) shows BB record holders ARE Collatz-like residue maps, so this is not an analogy: any WFAR upgrade that decides such machines is proving Collatz-like theorems. The honest boundary: Antihydra-style holdouts need invariants about the parity SEQUENCE of an orbit (essentially equidistribution), which no finite-dimensional counter certificate of this family can express — that boundary, not the certificate format, is where genuinely new mathematics would have to appear.
sources_read: web: arXiv:1111.3427, arXiv:2503.19561, Parikh automata surveys

translations.technique: Certificate search and synthesis layer: SAT encoding of MitM-DFA pairs with BFS-canonical symmetry breaking (mitm_dfa.rs), TNF-style dead-state enumeration of weighted automata (mitmwfar decider.go), and the 'certificate = seed for a verified closure' architecture
translations.what_it_actually_does: mitm_dfa.rs encodes 'there exists an n-state DFA pair (left/right tape classifiers) plus an accept relation on (qL, f, r, qR) tuples satisfying start/halt/closure conditions' as CNF for the CaDiCaL solver: DFA transition outcomes use an order encoding (variables for =k and <=k with sequential at-most-one constraints, citing Sinz CP-2005); closure conditions are 4-literal clauses per (rule, qL, qR, b, target states); halting rules force unit clauses accept(qL,f,r,qR); the initial tuple (0,0,0,0) is hardwired FALSE. Symmetry breaking: auxiliary running-maximum variables tmax[qb] encode that DFA states must appear in first-use order (BFS-canonical form), eliminating relabelings and unused states from the search space; variable indices are hand-packed via a lattice-point-counting function a(b,h). A solution yields only the left DFA; DirectProver::complete_unverified recomputes the NFA half by saturation and the result is re-checked by the trusted 8-condition verifier. mitmwfar's decider enumerates base DFA pairs closed under TM transitions in a TNF-like order (start with all transitions to an explicit dead state, branch on replacing dead transitions — isomorphic to how bbchallenge enumerates TMs in Tree Normal Form), bounds the number of live transitions, then for each base tries weighting one left/right transition pair with (+1,-1), derives special sets and accept set by widening closure, and calls the verifier. The '-m' option pre-composes both automata with addWFAMemory, whose states are the live transitions of the old automaton (line-graph construction), iterated m times = memory of the last m symbols.
translations.math_translation: The synthesis problem solved is: given a finite string-rewriting system R (the TM), decide whether there exists a separating automaton of size <= n — a regular (FAR) or one-counter-weighted (WFAR) set A with pre(A) ⊆ A (equivalently post-closed complement), Init ∉ A, Halt ⊆ A. This is a Sigma_1 statement over a finite space, encoded so that: (i) the canonicity predicate (tmax running-max clauses) selects exactly one representative per isomorphism class of trim DFAs, so SAT search runs over canonical forms — mathematically, imposing a BFS-order total ordering on reachable states; (ii) the certificate stored/verified is only a generator (precursor DFA, or WDFA pair + widening threshold): the invariant itself is the least fixpoint of a verified closure operator applied to the seed, recomputed at checking time. Implicit lemma: the completion map seed -> invariant is deterministic and its result, if the 8 (resp. Thm 4.10) conditions pass, is a valid certificate regardless of the untrusted search's correctness — a guess-and-check/certifying-algorithm decomposition where the 'proof object' has been compressed below the size of the proof.

nearest_known_math.theory: SAT-based exact DFA identification with BFS-based symmetry breaking
nearest_known_math.relationship: subsumed-by: order-encoded transition variables + clauses forcing states to appear in canonical (BFS) order is exactly the Heule-Verwer / Ulyantsev-Zakirzyanov-Shalyto methodology, independently re-derived (the code credits community members djmati1111/Mateon1, not this literature)
nearest_known_math.ref: Heule-Verwer, 'Exact DFA identification using SAT solvers', ICGI 2010; Ulyantsev et al., 'BFS-based symmetry breaking...', 2016

nearest_known_math.theory: Automata learning for verification / ICE invariant synthesis; separating automata
nearest_known_math.relationship: overlaps: guess-check loops for regular inductive invariants are established in regular model checking and parity game literature; here the checker is a Coq-verified reflection procedure
nearest_known_math.ref: Garg-Löding-Madhusudan-Neider ICE (CAV 2014); Vardhan et al., 'Learning to verify safety properties', 2004

nearest_known_math.theory: Proof by reflection / certifying algorithms with verified checkers
nearest_known_math.relationship: subsumed-by: untrusted search + verified checker + certificate-as-hint is the standard skeptical architecture (CeTA for termination, validsdp for SOS); the 'seed + verified closure' compression is a nice instance, not a new principle
nearest_known_math.ref: Thiemann-Sternagel, CeTA (TPHOLs 2009); Boutin, 'Using reflection to build efficient and certified decision procedures'

translations.fusion_opportunity: The WFAR search is the weak link: blind enumeration over base DFAs and +-1 weight placements (README: 'other weight pairs — no success'). Once the closure conditions are read as difference constraints over integer weight/bound variables (as established in the WFAR translation), the joint synthesis problem is exactly SAT modulo difference logic (structure Booleans choose automaton edges and accept tuples; integer variables carry weights and interval bounds; closure clauses become conditional difference constraints) — decidable and well-supported by SMT solvers, so one solver call replaces the enumerate-perturb-close loop and searches ALL integer weightings at once. At the SOS level, alternate SAT (discrete structure) with SDP (template coefficients) in a CEGIS loop, with counterexample configurations returned by the failed closure check. Additionally, the accidental rediscoveries should be made deliberate: use the termination community's existing SAT encodings for N/arctic matrix interpretations as the weight-search backend, and use the path-complete literature's ordering results (which expansions dominate which) to schedule the -m memory refinements instead of trying them ad hoc.
translations.collatz_application: Directly reusable for our certificate program: Yolcu-Aaronson-Heule already demonstrated the SAT half (rewriting encodes Collatz; SAT finds matrix/arctic interpretations for termination fragments). The missing piece their pipeline does not have and bbchallenge's does: seed-compressed, Coq-checkable safety certificates with a verified closure/widening checker. Concretely: implement a Collatz-rewriting WFAR searcher as SAT-modulo-difference-logic over (DWFA structure, Z^2 weights (odd-steps, halvings), interval bounds), with BFS-canonical symmetry breaking clauses ported from mitm_dfa.rs, and a Coq/Lean verifier that recomputes the accept set by widening closure from the seed — giving machine-checked lemmas of the form 'no n in regular family S reaches 1 (or re-enters S) under T' as composable building blocks for case-analysis proofs about Collatz-like maps.
sources_read: web: Heule-Verwer / Ulyantsev DFA-SAT search results

weird_unnoticed: The mitmwfar '-m' flag is an independent reinvention of De Bruijn path-complete graph expansions from switched-systems control theory, and nobody on either side has noticed. addWFAMemory (decider.go lines 343-383) rebuilds the weighted automaton with states = live transitions of the old automaton (the line graph), iterated m times = states remember the last m symbols read. In the path-complete Lyapunov literature this exact construction (De Bruijn expansion / path-dependent Lyapunov functions) is THE canonical way to strictly increase certificate power at fixed template degree, with theorems ordering the resulting hierarchies — while Iijil's README describes it only as an empirical knob ('-m can be added to transform the WA... to give them a memory'). The correspondence is exact because both sides are relaxing the same thing: per-node certificate functions indexed by a finite quotient of history. Two smaller unnoticed items: (1) the two 'equivalent' WFAR verifiers actually check different certificate classes — Go's accept set is a map from control tuple to at most ONE interval (convex per tuple), while Coq's is a finite set of (point | upward half-line | downward half-line) elements per tuple (nonconvex unions allowed); (2) the paper's Bellman-Ford story is aspirational — footnote 49 admits it, and reading both codebases confirms only the sign-definite fragment (backward-closed nonneg/nonpos state labelings) is implemented anywhere, and all 17 shipped Coq certificates use edge weights drawn from {-1,0,1} — the entire deployed power of 'weighted' FAR is one tropical unit.
dead_ends: docs.bbchallenge.org is unreachable from this network as warned, but was not needed — the deciders repo READMEs, the BB5 paper, Coq-BB5 sources, and mitmwfar sources cover everything. pypdf was missing and had to be installed to the user site to locate WFAR sections in the 48-page PDF (plain `Read` on page ranges then worked fine). I searched both codebases for a Bellman-Ford implementation to pin down the 'Bellman-Ford feasibility' claim from the task prompt: it does not exist in code (grep for negative-cycle / relaxation logic; only wdfa_sgn_closed in Coq and deriveSpecialSets/transitionRetainsSpecialSets in Go) — the task's 'Bellman-Ford feasibility for WFAR' description comes from the paper's in-principle remark, not the artifact. I also checked whether the WFAR search uses SAT (it does not — pure recursive enumeration; only FAR's mitm prover is SAT-based), and whether any shipped certificate uses weights beyond +-1 (none do; the handcrafted ~50-state 'Helices' certificates are bigger in states, not in weight alphabet). Finally, I looked for prior literature stating FAR's universality lemma (every regular co-CTL fits the Thm 4.9 normal form) in regular model checking terms and found completeness results of similar flavor but not that exact normal form — it stands as a small new lemma, not new theory, which kept me from grading anything 'plausibly-new-mathematics': every load-bearing component (regular invariants, saturation, Parikh/blind-counter languages, interval widening, difference-constraint potentials, DFA-SAT synthesis, path-complete templates) has an established home; the novelty is the assembly and the two ends of the semiring ladder they happened to build.
